idioms.definition: To avoid committing to an opinion or position; to remain neutral in a debate or argument
examples: He always sits on the fence when politics comes up.
Stop sitting on the fence and tell me what you actually think!
The government is accused of sitting on the fence on this issue.
idioms.usageTip: Often used critically to suggest someone is being indecisive or avoiding responsibility. Can also be neutral when describing someone who genuinely wants to stay uninvolved.

idioms.idiom: get the wrong end of the stick
idioms.definition: To misunderstand a situation completely; to have a completely incorrect understanding of something
examples: He got the wrong end of the stick and thought the event was on Friday.
I think you've got the wrong end of the stick – she wasn't criticising you.
She got the wrong end of the stick and was angry for no reason.
idioms.usageTip: Used to correct a misunderstanding gently or to explain that someone has misinterpreted the situation.

idioms.idiom: bury your head in the sand
idioms.definition: To deliberately ignore a problem or difficult situation, hoping it will go away on its own
examples: You can't just bury your head in the sand – you need to deal with this.
Companies that buried their heads in the sand about climate change are now facing consequences.
Burying your head in the sand won't make the debt disappear.
idioms.usageTip: Based on the (incorrect) belief that ostriches bury their heads to avoid danger. Always implies a negative attitude – avoidance of reality.

idioms.definition: To suddenly start expressing a very different opinion or attitude; to contradict what you previously said
examples: He used to say the plan was brilliant, but now he's changed his tune.
Once she heard the evidence, she quickly changed her tune.
It's funny how people change their tune when the situation affects them directly.
idioms.usageTip: Often implies that the change is influenced by self-interest or new information. Can be used critically or neutrally.

idioms.definition: Having the same understanding, goals, or expectations; in agreement about something
examples: Before we start, let's make sure we're all on the same page.
The two teams weren't on the same page about the timeline.
It's important for parents and teachers to be on the same page.
idioms.usageTip: Common in professional and collaborative contexts. Very similar to 'on the same wavelength', but 'on the same page' refers more to shared facts or plans, while 'wavelength' implies deeper understanding.

idioms.definition: To understand the general meaning or intention of what someone is saying
examples: I'm not saying you're wrong, but… you catch my drift.
He didn't finish the sentence, but she got his drift.
Even if they don't understand every word, they'll get the drift.
idioms.usageTip: 'Do you get my drift?' is a common way of checking if someone understood your implied meaning. Often used when the full message is left unsaid.

fillInExercises.sentence: The politician refused to give a direct answer – as usual, he just ___ on the issue.
options: buried his head in the sand
got the wrong end of the stick
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Sit on the fence' means to avoid taking a side or expressing a clear opinion.

fillInExercises.sentence: You've completely ___. I wasn't criticising your work – I was asking a genuine question.
options: got the wrong end of the stick
buried your head in the sand
fillInExercises.correct: got the wrong end of the stick
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get the wrong end of the stick' means to completely misunderstand a situation.

fillInExercises.sentence: You can't ___ about this financial problem. You need to face it head on.
options: bury your head in the sand
get the wrong end of the stick
fillInExercises.correct: bury your head in the sand
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bury your head in the sand' means to avoid or ignore a problem instead of dealing with it.

fillInExercises.sentence: Before the meeting starts, I want to make sure we're all ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On the same page' means having the same understanding or expectations.

fillInExercises.sentence: She was very much against the proposal, but once she got a bonus tied to it, she ___.
options: got the wrong end of the stick
buried her head in the sand
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Change your tune' means to suddenly adopt a very different opinion or attitude.

fillInExercises.sentence: I'm not going to spell it out – I think you ___, don't you?
options: bury your head in the sand
get the wrong end of the stick
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Catch/get sb's drift' means to understand the implied meaning of what someone is saying.

lines.speaker: Rachel
lines.text: So, does everyone agree with the new approach? Or are we going to [sit on the fence|sit on the fence] again?

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: I want to be clear – I'm not avoiding the issue. I genuinely think both options have merit.

lines.speaker: Rachel
lines.text: Fair enough. But we can't keep [burying our heads in the sand|bury your head in the sand] about the budget constraints.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: I think you've [got the wrong end of the stick|get the wrong end of the stick]. I'm not ignoring the budget – I just want a proper discussion.

lines.speaker: Rachel
lines.text: You're right. I'm sorry. Let's start fresh and make sure we're all [on the same page|on the same page].

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Exactly. And once we agree on the figures, I suspect Dan will [change his tune|change your tune] too.

lines.speaker: Rachel
lines.text: Ha – I think you [catch my drift|catch sb's drift] exactly. He always comes around eventually.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Let's set up a proper review for Thursday.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: I want to talk about the client feedback. I'm not going to sugarcoat it – you [catch my drift|catch sb's drift], I'm sure.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: Yes, I do. But I think some of the team [got the wrong end of the stick|get the wrong end of the stick] about what the client actually wanted.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: Maybe. But we can't [bury our heads in the sand|bury your head in the sand] about the fact that the delivery was late.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: Agreed. I take responsibility for that. I won't [sit on the fence|sit on the fence] – it was my team's mistake.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: I appreciate that. Let's make sure we're [on the same page|on the same page] going forward about timelines and expectations.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: Absolutely. And I noticed you [changed your tune|change your tune] on the deadline after the client called – was that intentional?

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.text: Yes – the situation changed, so I had to adapt. Good observation.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: avoid committing to an opinion or side

pairs.idiom: get the wrong end of the stick
bury your head in the sand
pairs.meaning: ignore a problem hoping it will go away
suddenly express a very different opinion
having the same understanding or agreement
understand the implied meaning of what is said

quizExercises.sentence: Before we finalise the contract, let's check that the legal team is ___.
options: burying their heads in the sand
quizExercises.explanation: 'On the same page' means everyone has the same understanding or expectations.

quizExercises.sentence: He used to be very critical of remote working, but once he tried it himself, he ___.
options: got the wrong end of the stick
quizExercises.explanation: 'Change your tune' means to suddenly hold a very different opinion than before.

quizExercises.idiom: bury your head in the sand
quizExercises.question: Which behaviour best describes 'burying your head in the sand'?
options: Researching a problem thoroughly before making a decision
Refusing to acknowledge a serious issue, hoping it resolves itself
Changing your opinion after receiving new information
quizExercises.explanation: 'Bury your head in the sand' describes deliberately ignoring a problem instead of facing it.

quizExercises.idiom: get the wrong end of the stick
quizExercises.question: If someone 'got the wrong end of the stick', what happened?
options: They understood exactly what was meant
They chose not to take part in an argument
quizExercises.explanation: 'Get the wrong end of the stick' means to have an entirely incorrect understanding of something.
